parentQuestion: A patient has a wound that is healing very slowly. Biopsy shows abundant granulation tissue that is failing to transition to the next phase of repair. Which phase is the tissue 'stuck' in?

scaffolds.q: Tissue repair happens in three phases: inflammation, proliferation, remodeling.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Each phase must complete before the next can succeed.

scaffolds.q: Phase 1 (inflammation) involves cleanup - immune cells remove debris.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! First responders clear the damage zone. Painful but necessary.

scaffolds.q: Phase 2 (proliferation) involves building - new tissue scaffold forms.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Fibroblasts and new blood vessels create granulation tissue.

scaffolds.q: GRANULATION TISSUE is characteristic of the PROLIFERATIVE phase.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! That pink, granular tissue with new capillaries = proliferation is happening.

scaffolds.q: Phase 3 (remodeling) involves maturation - collagen reorganizes, scar strengthens.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The final phase refines the repair - can take months or years.

scaffolds.q: If you see GRANULATION TISSUE, the wound is in the PROLIFERATIVE phase.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Granulation tissue is THE hallmark of proliferation.

scaffolds.q: A wound stuck WITH granulation tissue is stuck IN proliferation.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The tissue can't transition to remodeling. Construction continues but never finishes.

scaffolds.q: This wound shows granulation tissue, so it's stuck in the PROLIFERATIVE phase.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Not inflammatory (that's before granulation), not remodeling (that's after). Proliferation = granulation.

scaffolds.q: The answer is PROLIFERATIVE phase - granulation tissue is its hallmark.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The wound keeps making scaffold tissue but can't progress to final remodeling/healing.
